Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it searches, ranks and compresses large doc sets into a small, highly relevant context (≈90% reduction) that clients pass to their own LLMs—enabling accurate LLM responses within token limits without vendor lock‑in or Snipara paying LLM inference costs.

Key points:
- ~90% context reduction (e.g., 500K → ~5K tokens)
- Returns optimized context (not LLM responses) for any client LLM (LLM‑agnostic)
- Token budgeting, session persistence, and recursive decomposition for very large docs
- Low cost/high margins (no provider LLM inference fees) and team/shared‑context features for consistent behavior across projects

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it finds, ranks and compresses the most relevant pieces of large documentation so clients can use their own LLMs to produce accurate results within strict token limits.

Key points:
- Dramatic context reduction (≈90%): compresses large docs (e.g., 500K tokens) down to a small, highly relevant context (≈5K tokens).
- LLM-agnostic: clients use any LLM (Claude, GPT, Gemini, local models) — no vendor lock‑in.
- No LLM inference costs for Snipara: only pay for context optimization (high margins).
- Token budgeting and precise control (max_tokens) so returned context fits the client’s model window.
- Session persistence, shared team collections, and recursive decomposition enable near‑infinite/long‑running context workflows.
- MCP-native: designed to plug directly into clients’ LLM workflows via the Model Context Protocol.

Snipara’s core value proposition is Context Optimization as a Service: it finds and returns the smallest, most relevant slice of documentation so clients’ own LLMs can answer complex queries within token limits. Key points:

- ~90% context reduction (e.g., 500K tokens → ~5K highly relevant tokens)  
- Token budgeting and smart chunking to fit client max_tokens limits  
- LLM-agnostic — clients use their own LLM (no vendor lock‑in, no inference costs for Snipara)  
- Session persistence and recursive decomposition for near‑infinite context handling

Put simply: Snipara delivers optimized context (not LLM answers), enabling accurate LLM results with far fewer tokens and lower cost.

Snipara is split into three cooperating components — each has a clear responsibility, deployment target, and repo:

1) Client (snipara-mcp)
- Purpose: Lightweight client/SDK that callers install (pip) to talk to an MCP server and expose Snipara tools to local runtimes/agents.
- Responsibilities: Authenticate (X-API-Key / OAuth), call MCP endpoints (JSON-RPC / tools/call), provide stdio/http transports for agents.
- Tech / deploy: Python package on PyPI (snipara-mcp). Config via SNIPARA_API_KEY, SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID, SNIPARA_API_URL.
- Interaction: Sends HTTP requests to the MCP server (e.g., POST https://api.snipara.com/mcp/{project}).

2) Backend MCP Server (snipara-fastapi / apps/mcp-server)
- Purpose: The core context-optimization and agent engine that runs tools (rlm_engine), validates keys, enforces rate/usage limits, and returns optimized context/results.
- Responsibilities: API endpoints (POST /v1/{project}/mcp, /health, /v1/{project}/context, /license), API key & OAuth auth, usage tracking, Redis rate-limits & caches, vector storage/pgvector, embeddings, token counting, and tool execution.
- Tech / deploy: FastAPI (Python 3.12), deployed as api.snipara.com on Railway; private repo snipara-fastapi (plus source in the monorepo under apps/mcp-server). Uses Postgres (Neon), Redis, pgvector, tiktoken, sentence-transformers.
- Important operational note: Changes to apps/mcp-server/src/ must be synced (dual-repo push) into snipara-fastapi and deployed (use Railway CLI `railway up` — Git auto-deploy is flaky).

3) Web app / Console (apps/web)
- Purpose: Marketing site, dashboard and management UI for projects, keys, billing, license/trial, docs and self-hosting guides.
- Responsibilities: User-facing pages (marketing, self-hosting docs, billing, agents dashboard), Sentry integration, license/trial flows, onboarding and links to MCP usage.
- Tech / deploy: Next.js app in the monorepo, typically deployed to Vercel (with environment variables and Sentry). Source lives in the monorepo (RLMSaas).

How they fit together
- Users interact with the Web app for onboarding, license/key issuance and documentation.
- Clients (snipara-mcp or local stdio transports) call the Backend MCP Server (api.snipara.com) to run tools and fetch optimized context.
- Backend uses Redis/postgres/pgvector and the RLM engine to perform semantic search, token budgeting, and tool execution; it returns results and usage metrics to the client.
- Operations: MCP server code is kept in the monorepo and must be pushed/synced to the separate snipara-fastapi repo for deployment; deployments to Railway should use the CLI (`railway up`).

This separation keeps the runtime engine private and scalable, exposes a simple client SDK for integrations, and provides a web UI for users and administration.

Short answer
Snipara enforces a predictable, relevance-first token budget when returning context to an LLM. It counts real tokens (not chars), ranks candidate sections by relevance, and greedily selects content until the max_tokens budget is exhausted — truncating sections safely at sentence boundaries if needed.

How it works (step‑by‑step)
- Token counting: uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to count tokens for every candidate section and for truncation calculations.
- Entry point: rlm_context_query (query, max_tokens, search_mode, include_metadata, session_id, …).
- Search modes: keyword, semantic, or hybrid to produce candidate sections.
- Relevance ranking: candidates are scored and sorted (multi-factor ranking).
- Greedy selection: pick highest‑relevance sections first; add full sections while total_tokens + section_tokens ≤ max_tokens.
- Partial fits: if a section doesn’t fully fit, smart_truncate() trims to the remaining token allowance at the nearest sentence boundary (. ! ?), appends "…", and marks the section as truncated.
- Metadata: each returned section includes title, content, file path, line ranges, relevance_score, and token_count. The overall response includes total_tokens, truncated flag, session_context (if any), and suggestions.
- Session & system context: session context can be injected (limited — e.g., up to ~20% of budget) and system instructions are auto-injected as part of budgeting considerations.
- Budget allocation across sources/categories: project docs are prioritized (default ~40% of budget). Shared collections are allocated by category using a TokenBudget (defaults from implementation: project 0.40, mandatory 0.25, best_practices 0.20, guidelines 0.10, reference 0.05), and docs within each group are allocated greedily by relevance until that group’s token slice is used.
- Limits & safeguards: hard limits are enforced (e.g., max tokens per collection, max docs per collection, minimum relevance threshold) to avoid extreme results.
- Metrics & tooling: the system reports search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache hit, and token efficiency metrics (compression_ratio, token_reduction_pct, cost_savings_pct). There are also helpers for multi-query execution (per-query budgets) and decomposition of complex queries into sub‑queries without calling an LLM.

Why this design
- Accurate token accounting (tiktoken) avoids surprises in LLM billing and responses.
- Relevance‑first, greedy allocation maximizes useful context given a strict token cap.
- Smart truncation preserves sentence boundaries for readability and LLM friendliness.
- Category/project allocations ensure critical rules (e.g., mandatory, project‑specific) are prioritized.

Example response shape (conceptual)
- sections: [{ title, content, file, lines, relevance_score, token_count, truncated? }, …]
- total_tokens: <used tokens>
- truncated: true|false
- session_context: optional
- suggestions: [ … ]

If you want, I can show a short walkthrough of how a sample query would be processed (search → rank → allocate → truncate → response) with numbers to illustrate token accounting.

Short answer
- Snipara enforces a hard token budget (max_tokens, default 4000) and returns an optimized, metadata-rich set of document sections that fit inside that budget.
- It counts tokens with tiktoken (cl100k_base), ranks candidate sections by relevance, then greedily selects highest-relevance content until the token budget is used. If only part of a section fits, it performs a sentence-aware "smart truncate" and marks that section as truncated.

How it works (step‑by‑step)

1. Budget composition
- A total token budget is provided (max_tokens, e.g. 4000).
- The system uses a token-allocation policy to split that budget among sources/categories. Default allocations used across the codebase:
  - project: 0.40 (40%)
  - MANDATORY (shared): 0.25 (25%)
  - BEST_PRACTICES (shared): 0.20 (20%)
  - GUIDELINES (shared): 0.10 (10%)
  - REFERENCE (shared): 0.05 (5%)
- These per‑category budgets are applied when merging project + shared collections so higher priority content is guaranteed a slice of the budget.

2. Candidate retrieval & scoring
- The system can search in keyword, semantic, or hybrid mode.
- Documents/sections are returned as candidates and scored for relevance (relevance_score).
- Candidate pools are often grouped by source/category (project vs shared categories).

3. Token counting
- Token counts are measured with tiktoken using the cl100k_base encoder (same encoding as modern OpenAI/Claude models), so token accounting matches the LLMs' tokenization.
- Each section/document has a computed token_count.

4. Selection algorithm
- There are two complementary selection behaviors in the code:
  - Merged allocation path: allocate each category its share of tokens (group_budget = total_budget * allocation), then select top-relevance docs within each group until that group_budget is exhausted.
  - rlm_context_query path: rank all matching sections by relevance and greedily pick highest-relevance sections until the overall max_tokens is reached.
- In either case the selection is greedy by relevance and stops when adding another full section would exceed the allowed tokens.

5. Smart truncation
- If a section would partially fit, Snipara runs smart_truncate():
  - Truncates to the remaining token budget.
  - Cuts on sentence boundaries (period, exclamation, question) to avoid chopping mid-sentence.
  - Appends "..." and flags the section as truncated in the metadata.
  - The response-level truncated flag is set if any truncation or budget cap is reached.

6. Response metadata
- Returned sections include: title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated (if applicable).
- The overall response contains sections[], total_tokens used, truncated boolean, session_context (if any), and suggestions (e.g., "Also check: security.md").
- Tools also return diagnostic metrics: search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit, etc.

7. Session & system considerations
- Session context can be injected (implementation notes: allowed up to ~20% of the budget).
- System instructions may be auto-injected into the context where required.
- Caching/versioning is used for merged context to avoid repeated work.

Example (numbers)

High-level summary — how Snipara’s token budgeting works

- Token counting
  - Uses tiktoken (cl100k_base) to count tokens precisely (not chars/words).
  - All candidate sections are measured in tokens up-front so selection is deterministic.

- Per-query budget
  - Each context query accepts max_tokens (default 4000). That is the hard token budget for returned context.
  - Session context can be included (controlled/limited — e.g., up to ~20% of the budget).

- Search & candidate generation
  - Query can be run in keyword, semantic, or hybrid mode.
  - The engine returns candidate sections with relevance scores and token counts (metadata collected during search).

- Ranking & allocation
  - Candidates are ranked by relevance (multi-factor ranking).
  - There are two allocation strategies in play:
    - Global greedy selection: iterate candidates (highest relevance first), add whole sections until budget would be exceeded; if a section would overflow, try partial inclusion via smart truncation.
    - Category/group allocation (merged contexts): budgets are split by source/category using default allocations and priorities (project first). Default allocations:
      - project: 40%
      - MANDATORY: 25%
      - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
      - GUIDELINES: 10%
      - REFERENCE: 5%
    - Within each group, items are sorted by relevance and included until that group’s budget is used.

- Smart truncation (smart_truncate)
  - If only part of a section fits, the engine truncates at a sentence boundary (. ! ?), appends "..." and marks that section as truncated.
  - The truncation uses token counts (via the encoder) to fit the remaining tokens precisely.

- Response metadata
  - Each returned section includes: title, content, file path, line range, relevance_score, token_count, and truncated flag when applicable.
  - Top-level response includes total_tokens (used), truncated (true if budget filled), session_context (if any), and suggestions.
  - Search usage info is returned separately (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit).

- Performance & safety features
  - Merged/project context is cached (version-hash based) to reduce repeated work.
  - Hard limits are enforced (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, docs per collection).
  - Tools support decomposition and multi-query execution with per-query budgets.

- Typical flow (request → result)
  1. Client calls rlm_context_query(query, max_tokens, search_mode, include_metadata).
  2. Engine gathers candidates (keyword/semantic/hybrid), counts tokens per candidate.
  3. Engine ranks candidates and greedily fills the budget (or allocates by category if using merged context budgeting).
  4. If needed, smart_truncate is used to fit the last section.
  5. Engine returns sections with metadata + totals and usage info.

Why this matters
- Precise token counting and prioritized budget allocation let Snipara return the most relevant context within a fixed token budget, improving LLM prompt efficiency and reducing cost while preserving important information.

Short answer
- Shared context collections are team-level repositories of reusable docs (coding standards, security rules, templates, examples) that projects can link to so every project and AI query automatically inherits the team’s rules and guidance.

How they work (overview)
1. Create a collection (team admin/owner) and upload documents (coding standards, templates, guidelines).
2. Link that collection to one or more projects (dashboard or API). Linked projects automatically inherit the collection’s documents.
3. When you run a documentation/code query (e.g., rlm_context_query), the platform merges the shared context with project docs and returns combined results. Shared context is consulted first and allocated a portion of the token budget.
4. The AI then uses the merged context to produce outputs that follow your team’s standards.

Key concepts
- Categories and priority: Documents are categorized and prioritized. Typical categories (and their default token-budget targets) are:
  - MANDATORY (highest) — 40% of shared budget (non-negotiable rules: security, architecture)
  - BEST_PRACTICES — 30% (recommended patterns)
  - GUIDELINES — 20% (helpful suggestions)
  - REFERENCE — 10% (background/examples)
  Within a category you can assign a numeric priority (0–100) to emphasize importance.
- Token budget: Shared context is allocated a portion of the query token budget (defaults vary; examples show 30–40% to shared context and the rest to project docs). You can control this (e.g., include_shared_context, shared_context_budget_percent, max_tokens).
- Merge order: Shared context is merged and returned first, then project-specific docs—so mandatory team rules appear before local docs.
- Inheritance: Linking a collection causes all linked projects to automatically inherit its documents; you can reorder linked collections to change priority.
- Templates: Collections can contain prompt templates (access via rlm_list_templates and rlm_get_template) for consistent reviews, specs, PR checks, etc.

Primary tools / APIs
- rlm_shared_context — retrieve merged shared context for use before implementing or inject into sessions.
- rlm_list_collections — list available collections and get IDs.
- rlm_upload_shared_document — upload or update documents in a collection programmatically.
- rlm_list_templates / rlm_get_template — list and render prompt templates.
- rlm_multi_project_query — search across all team projects to find implementations or patterns.
- Team Context API endpoints (POST /api/teams/:teamId/context, GET /api/teams/:teamId/context, PUT/PATCH/DELETE) to manage contexts programmatically.

Permissions & plans
- Team/shared-context features require a Team (or Enterprise) plan. Creating shared contexts usually requires ADMIN/OWNER role.
- Some tools (rlm_shared_context, templates, multi-project queries) are Team+ or Enterprise features.

Typical workflow (example)
1. Team admin creates a collection and uploads MANDATORY rules and BEST_PRACTICES (rlm_upload_shared_document or dashboard).
2. Link the collection to projects via Project > Shared Context or API.
3. Developers call rlm_shared_context (or rlm_context_query with include_shared_context) to load standards before implementing.
4. Inject those standards into the session (rlm_inject) so subsequent queries must follow them.
5. Use rlm_multi_project_query to discover existing implementations across the team.
6. Update collections as standards evolve; projects automatically receive the updates.

Best practices
- Put security/architecture rules in MANDATORY.
- Keep documents focused (one topic per doc).
- Use priorities to resolve conflicts.
- Provide templates for code reviews and PR checks.
- Update collections regularly and track changes.

Summary — what happens (high level)
- Snipara implements the OAuth 2.0 Device Flow (RFC 8628) so CLI/desktop clients can authenticate via a browser without copying API keys.
- User runs a local CLI command → server returns a short user_code and a verification URL → user opens the URL (or uses verification_uri_complete) and signs in → client polls the token endpoint until the user completes authorization → server returns an access token (and refresh token).
- Access tokens are used with Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...; clients prefer stored OAuth tokens but will fall back to project API keys if no valid OAuth token exists.

Step-by-step (detailed)
1. Start device authorization
   - Client calls POST /api/oauth/device/code with body:
     { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
   - Server response includes:
     - device_code (opaque token the client will later poll with)
     - user_code (short code user types in)
     - verification_uri (e.g. https://snipara.com/device)
     - verification_uri_complete (link embedding the user_code)
     - expires_in (e.g. 900 seconds)
     - interval (recommended poll interval, e.g. 5 seconds)

2. User authorizes
   - User visits verification_uri (or clicks verification_uri_complete), signs in, and approves the device using the user_code / embedded link.

3. Client polls for token
   - Client repeatedly POSTs /api/oauth/device/token with:
     {
       "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
       "device_code": "<device_code>",
       "client_id": "snipara_cli"
     }
   - While awaiting user action server returns { "error": "authorization_pending" }.
   - After approval server returns on success:
     {
       "access_token": "snipara_at_...",
       "token_type": "Bearer",
       "expires_in": 2592000,
       "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..."
     }

4. Token usage and storage
   - Access tokens are sent as Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
   - The client typically stores tokens locally (e.g. ~/.snipara/tokens.json) for reuse.
   - Snipara validates tokens (DB lookup + expiry check — code references validate_oauth_token()) and enforces scopes.

Server/client behavior and policies
- Authentication priority: client checks for a stored OAuth token (~/.snipara/tokens.json) first; if none or invalid, it falls back to API key from environment variables.
- Token format: OAuth access tokens use the snipara_at_ prefix in the system.
- Scopes: enforced on server (read/write scope enforcement).
- New device login: the system can notify users (e.g., sendNewDeviceLoginEmail) when sign-in is detected from a new device.

Troubleshooting and alternatives
- If OAuth tokens are expired or you want to force API-key auth:
  - Set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client environment to skip token discovery and use the API key.
  - Or remove/backup ~/.snipara/tokens.json to force API key fallback.
- Common error: "authorization_pending" — meaning the client should keep polling until the user completes authorization.

Headers and API key alternative
- OAuth header: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- API key header: X-API-Key: rlm_...
- Clients that support both will choose OAuth token first, then API key.

Example minimal sequence (requests)
- Start device flow:
  POST /api/oauth/device/code
  Body: { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
- Poll for token:
  POST /api/oauth/device/token
  Body: {
    "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code",
    "device_code": "<device_code>",
    "client_id": "snipara_cli"
  }

Summary — OAuth Device Flow in Snipara (RFC 8628)
- Purpose: let CLI users authenticate without pasting API keys. The CLI asks the user to visit a browser URL and enter a short code; the CLI polls the server until the user authorizes and an access token is issued.

Step-by-step flow
1. Client starts device flow
   - POST /api/oauth/device/code with body: { "client_id": "snipara_cli" }.
   - Server responds with:
     - device_code (server-side code the client polls with)
     - user_code (short code the user types into the browser)
     - verification_uri (URL where user enters code)
     - verification_uri_complete (URL with code prefilled)
     - expires_in (e.g., 900 seconds)
     - interval (polling interval, e.g., 5s)

2. User authorizes in browser
   - User visits verification_uri (or verification_uri_complete) and signs in/authorizes the CLI using user_code.

3. Client polls for token
   - Repeatedly POST /api/oauth/device/token:
     - body: { "grant_type": "urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:device_code", "device_code": "<device_code>", "client_id": "snipara_cli" }
   - Server responses:
     - While waiting: { "error": "authorization_pending" } (or other RFC-8628 errors)
     - On success: { "access_token": "snipara_at_...", "token_type": "Bearer", "expires_in": <seconds>, "refresh_token": "snipara_rt_..." }

Token details and validation
- Access tokens use prefix snipara_at_ and refresh tokens snipara_rt_.
- Tokens are validated via DB lookup + expiry check (see validate_oauth_token()) and scopes (read/write) are enforced.
- Typical grant lifetime shown in docs (access/expires) and refresh tokens are returned for long-lived access.

Client behavior and storage
- Successful tokens are stored in ~/.snipara/tokens.json and discovered by MCP clients.
- MCP client authentication priority:
  1. Use stored OAuth token (Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...)
  2. Fall back to project/team API key (X-API-Key: rlm_...)
- You can force API-key-only behavior by:
  - Setting SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true in the MCP client env to skip tokens discovery.
  - Or removing ~/.snipara/tokens.json (rm or mv the file) to force API-key usage.

Common server responses / errors
- authorization_pending — user hasn’t completed authorization yet; continue polling per interval.
- If device_code expires (expires_in), client must restart the device flow.
- If the user authorizes, polling returns the access token JSON shown above.

Practical notes
- Device code expiry in examples: 900s (15 minutes).
- Poll interval example: 5 seconds.
- The server implements standard RFC 8628 device flow endpoints: POST /api/oauth/device/code and POST /api/oauth/device/token.
- Use Authorization: Bearer <access_token> when calling MCP endpoints after device flow completes.

Snipara uses a strict layered (vertical) architecture where each layer has a single responsibility and dependency flows downward only. Key points:

Layers (top → bottom)
- Routes (app/api/) — Thin HTTP handlers. Authenticate, validate (Zod), delegate to service/query, return responses. Must not contain business logic or DB calls.
- Jobs / Orchestrators (lib/jobs/) — Processors and workflow orchestration invoked by routes or background jobs.
- Services (lib/services/) — External API adapters and domain services (e.g., Stripe, LLM adapters). Use lazy init/retry for external clients.
- Repositories (lib/db/queries/) — Centralized CRUD only (repository pattern). All DB access goes here; route handlers must never call Prisma directly.
- Utils & Types (types/, lib/utils/) — Pure helpers and type definitions. Must not import any other layer.

Rules
- Dependencies flow downward only: higher layers may import lower layers, lower layers must not import higher layers.
- types/ and utils/ have no dependencies on other layers.
- Route handlers must be thin and follow the pattern: requireAuth → parse/validate input → call service/repository → return successResponse (with try/catch → handleError).
- Repository functions must enforce permission checks.
- Import ordering: external packages → internal packages → absolute (@/*) → relative.

This enforces separation of concerns, testability, and consistent import boundaries across the codebase.

Short answer
- Snipara automatically compresses a huge codebase down to a small, highly relevant context bundle your LLM can actually consume. In practice customers see reductions on the order of 90–99% (500K tokens → ~5K relevant tokens is a realistic example).
- That means your 128K-window model easily fits the needed context (plus instructions and model output) and you pay only for a tiny fraction of the tokens you’d otherwise try to shove into prompts — typically an order-of-magnitude or two of cost reduction per call.

How Snipara achieves this (practical steps)
1. Index + search
   - The repo is indexed (keyword + semantic embeddings). Searches return candidate sections instead of raw files.
2. Rank & budget
   - Candidates are scored for relevance and then greedily selected to fit a token budget you set (max_tokens). Snipara uses tiktoken-style counting and smart truncation (cut at sentence boundaries) when partial sections are needed.
3. Shared & session context
   - Team/shared documents (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, etc.) can be loaded first with a configurable percent of the budget (defaults like 30%), and session context can be persisted and allocated (e.g., up to ~20% of budget).
4. Recursive decomposition (if needed)
   - For questions that truly need large parts of the codebase, Snipara will decompose into sub-queries (rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query) and stitch results — enabling near‑infinite context without blasting the LLM with 500K tokens.
5. Caching & embedding reuse
   - Embeddings and merged context are cached (Redis/pgvector), so repeated queries are cheap.

Concrete cost-savings example (hypothetical pricing for illustration)
- Assumptions:
  - You have a 500K-token codebase.
  - Snipara selects ~5K tokens of relevant context.
  - Example LLM price: $0.03 per 1K input tokens (replace with your model’s price).

- Without Snipara (naïve):
  - If you somehow attempted to include 500K tokens (or had to make many large calls), token cost per request ~ 500 * $0.03 = $15.00.

- With Snipara:
  - Prompt tokens ~ 5K → cost per request ~ 5 * $0.03 = $0.15.

- Savings:
  - Absolute: ~$14.85 per request in this example.
  - Relative: ~99% per-call token-cost reduction (or a 100× reduction in tokens consumed).
  - Note: actual savings vary by model price, how many completions you request, and whether you pay for embeddings/search. Embedding costs are typically a one-time/periodic cost and are amortized across many queries.

Other practical benefits beyond raw dollars
- Fits within a 128K window with room for system prompts and output (no context overflow).
- Faster responses (less data passed to and processed by the LLM).
- Less hallucination and higher relevance because the model only receives targeted, high‑relevance context.
- Team rules and mandatory docs can be enforced automatically (shared context first).
- Fewer model calls overall when using recursive plans and cached searches.

What to do next
- Start by running rlm_context_query with max_tokens tuned to your needs (e.g., 4–8K).
- Measure actual token usage with tiktoken (cl100k_base) and multiply by your model’s per‑1K price to compute real savings.
- If deep-dive queries are common, enable recursive tools (rlm_decompose + rlm_multi_query) so you can synthesize large results efficiently.
- Monitor and cache heavy queries/embeddings to further reduce cost.

- rlm_decompose (use when complexity is high)

  - Example: “Explain the complete authentication system across microservices (login flow, JWT generation/validation, session storage, revocation)” → use rlm_decompose to get sub‑queries like “login flow”, “JWT generation”, “session management”, then run them in sequence/parallel.

Practical workflow
1. Try rlm_context_query first for a quick, authoritative answer.
2. If the result is incomplete or the topic is large/multi‑faceted:

Here are the authentication options present in the system (from the provided context), what they are, how they’re used, and their relative security trade‑offs and recommendations.

1) Project API Keys
- What: Long-lived keys scoped to a single project (formats: rlm_pk_..., rlm_sk_...).
- Usage: Sent as X-API-Key header to MCP and Inject APIs.
- Storage: Server stores SHA‑256 hash of the key; dashboard shows only prefix.
- Lifetime / revocation: Can be created with expiration, and can be revoked (soft delete).
- Security pros: Easy for automated clients/CI; server does not store raw key.
- Security cons: Broad access to project until rotated/revoked; if leaked, attacker has immediate access.
- Recommendations: Use for automation where project-scoped access is required; limit expiration, store in secret manager, rotate regularly, restrict permissions when possible.

2) Team API Keys
- What: Keys that grant access to all projects in a team (format: rlm_team_...).
- Usage: Same header (X-API-Key) but covers multiple projects.
- Security tradeoffs: Very powerful—convenient for multi‑project automation but higher blast radius if compromised.
- Recommendations: Only create for trusted CI or ops accounts, enforce admin controls, rotate, audit last-used, use least-privilege mapping where possible.

3) OAuth Access Tokens (Device Flow)
- What: Short‑lived access tokens for user auth (snipara_at_...); refresh tokens (snipara_rt_...) to obtain new access tokens. Device flow (RFC 8628) supports CLI/browser sign‑in.
- Usage: Authorization: Bearer snipara_at_...
- Lifetime / refresh: Access tokens typically ~1 hour; refresh tokens ~30 days. Tokens can be revoked.
- Storage: CLI stores tokens in ~/.snipara/tokens.json (0600). Server may also cache tokens in process.
- Security pros: Short-lived tokens limit window of abuse; user-level scopes, revocation, and automatic refresh reduce need to embed long‑lived secrets in automation.
- Security cons: If refresh token or stored tokens are compromised, attacker can mint access tokens until refresh expires or is revoked. Client-side storage must be protected.
- Recommendations: Prefer OAuth for interactive user authentication, use device flow for CLIs, protect token files (0600), revoke compromised tokens, and restart processes to clear cached creds when rotating or removing tokens.

4) OAuth via Identity Providers / NextAuth (GitHub, Google, Auth0, Email magic links)
- What: Web-based sign-in providers for user accounts; NextAuth used for sessions and account linking.
- Usage: Web session cookies or JWTs for browser/API interaction; callbacks and scopes controlled by provider.
- Security pros: Delegates auth to trusted providers, supports 2FA at provider level; avoids sharing raw API keys with users.
- Security cons: Complexity around account linking (noted "dangerous email account linking" in audit). Security depends on provider configuration and callback URLs.
- Recommendations: Harden account linking flows, validate callbacks and state, and monitor provider scopes. Use provider 2FA/enforced security policies.

5) Header precedence & transport differences
- What: The server accepts either X-API-Key (preferred) or Authorization: Bearer, and supports both MCP (HTTP MCP) and Inject REST endpoints.
- Security notes: Prefer explicit X-API-Key for key-based auth; bearer header reserved for OAuth tokens. Ensure HTTPS is enforced to protect headers.

6) Server-side options to force API key use (operational controls)
- SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH environment variable: set to true to skip token discovery and force API key authentication (useful for automated environments).
- Removing ~/.snipara/tokens.json: forces client to fall back to API key, but server processes may cache tokens—restart required.
- Security implication: For production automation, explicitly disabling OAuth discovery reduces accidental use of user tokens but be careful not to disable security best practices.

7) Revocation and management endpoints
- Revoke single/all tokens via API (/api/oauth/revoke, DELETE /api/settings/tokens).
- Token management UI (Settings → MCP Tokens) to view/revoke tokens and audit last used.
- Security recommendation: Use these controls immediately on suspected compromise.

Security comparison summary
- Most secure (for interactive user flows): OAuth short‑lived access tokens + refresh cycle, with provider controls and revocation—reduces exposure from long-lived secrets.
- Best for automation but moderate risk: Project API keys—simple, stable, but long‑lived unless given expiry; good if stored safely and rotated.
- Highest risk (largest blast radius): Team API keys—powerful and should be tightly controlled.
- Web/social logins: Secure if identity provider is properly configured, but watch account linking and callback handling.
- Operational controls: Environment flags (SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH) and token deletion help manage which method is used and mitigate cached credentials—but require operational discipline (restarts, rotation).

Practical recommendations
- For human sign‑in and interactive CLI: use OAuth device flow.
- For automation/CI: use project API keys with short expiration and store in secret managers.
- For multi‑project automation: only use team keys if necessary; prefer scoped service accounts where possible.
- Rotate and revoke keys/tokens regularly; enable auditing and last‑used monitoring.
- Protect local token files (0600) and restart servers after rotating/removing tokens to clear caches.
- Use HTTPS everywhere; prefer least‑privilege and short lifetimes.

Available authentication options

- Project API Key
  - Format: rlm_pk_... (also legacy rlm_sk_...)
  - Use: Single-project machine access (MCP and REST)
  - How it’s sent: X-API-Key header (preferred) or in .mcp.json headers
  - Security characteristics:
    - Long-lived until revoked or expires (can be created with expiry)
    - Stored hashed (SHA‑256) in DB — original key shown only once and only prefix is stored/shown
    - No per-request short expiry; compromise grants continued access until rotation/revocation
    - Good for CI/CD and server-to-server automation where you control secret storage

- Team API Key
  - Format: rlm_team_...
  - Use: Single key covering all projects in a team
  - How it’s sent: X-API-Key header
  - Security characteristics:
    - Broader scope (access multiple projects) → higher blast radius
    - Same long-lived behavior as project keys
    - Use only when needed; restrict permissions and rotate frequently

- OAuth (Device Flow) — user-scoped access tokens
  - Format: snipara_at_... (access), snipara_rt_... (refresh)
  - Use: User authentication via device flow (RFC 8628) for CLI/browser pairing
  - How it’s sent: Authorization: Bearer <access_token>
  - Security characteristics:
    - Short-lived access tokens (e.g., 1 hour) + refresh tokens (e.g., 30 days)
    - Supports scopes, per-user context, can be tied to projects or users
    - Easier to revoke per-user tokens; tokens can be listed/revoked in Settings → MCP Tokens
    - Safer for interactive user sessions and operations that require user identity
    - Client stores tokens (e.g., ~/.snipara/tokens.json, file mode 0600) — protect that file
    - Server/client may cache tokens; you may need to restart processes to clear cached credentials

- OAuth via NextAuth / Social Providers (GitHub, Google) and Email magic links
  - Use: Web login flows, account linking, issuing sessions/JWTs
  - Security characteristics:
    - Standard OAuth protections, provider-backed identity
    - Session/JWT-based; suitable for web UI and interactive flows
    - Supports account linking and role-based access controls

- Auth0 / other OAuth providers
  - Use: External identity provider integration
  - Security characteristics: depends on provider; supports centralized identity and fine-grained policies

- “Missing auth” and header behavior
  - Header priority implemented: X-API-Key (preferred) → Authorization: Bearer → OAuth tokens
  - If no auth provided, endpoints return a guided 401 with onboarding steps

How they differ in security (summary)

- Scope & least privilege
  - OAuth tokens support scopes and user context — better for least-privilege, user-specific operations.
  - API keys (project or team) typically grant direct project/team-level access without per-request scopes.

- Lifetime & revocation
  - OAuth: short-lived access tokens + refresh tokens → lower risk if access token leaked; can revoke individual tokens.
  - API keys: longer-lived; compromise requires rotation/revocation to block access.

- Blast radius
  - Team API keys have the largest blast radius (access to many projects).
  - Project API keys are narrower.
  - OAuth tokens are per-user and can be limited to specific projects or scopes.

- Storage & server-side protection
  - API keys are hashed in DB (good for server storage hygiene).
  - OAuth refresh tokens are stored (e.g., tokens.json) and must be protected on client machines.
  - Always use secure file permissions (e.g., 0600) and environment variables for CI.

- Usability vs security trade-offs
  - API keys are simple and convenient for automation.
  - OAuth device flow is more secure for interactive users and avoids copy-pasting long keys.

Recommendations / best practices

- For automated systems/CI: use project API keys with minimal required privileges; store them in secret managers; rotate regularly.
- For cross-project automation where one key is needed: use team key only when necessary and monitor usage.
- For developer CLI and interactive user flows: prefer OAuth device flow (short-lived tokens + easy revoke).
- Protect token storage: use secure permissions, environment variables, or secrets stores; never commit keys.
- Monitor and revoke: list tokens and revoke compromised keys/tokens immediately; prefer short expiry where possible.
- Use least privilege and rotate keys; enable auditing and logging for API key usage.
- If you want to force API key usage on a client that would otherwise use cached OAuth tokens, set SNIPARA_IGNORE_OAUTH=true or remove ~/.snipara/tokens.json and restart the client.

Short answer
- The system splits the total token budget between project docs and shared collections, then divides the shared portion among the four shared categories (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE) according to configured percentages. Documents are included in each bucket by relevance until that bucket’s token sub-budget is exhausted.

Details / how it works
1. Two-layer allocation
   - Project vs shared: Project-specific docs are allocated a fixed portion first (so project docs get priority).
   - Shared categories: The remaining budget is split across the shared categories and each category is filled independently.

2. Default numbers (implementation defaults)
   - The code’s DEFAULT_ALLOCATION is:
     - project: 40% of total budget
     - MANDATORY: 25%
     - BEST_PRACTICES: 20%
     - GUIDELINES: 10%
     - REFERENCE: 5%
   - (Note: documentation elsewhere describes shared-category weights as 40/30/20/10 for MANDATORY/BEST_PRACTICES/GUIDELINES/REFERENCE — that is a conceptual rule; the concrete allocation used by the token-budget implementation is above and can be overridden.)

3. Configurability
   - rlm_context_query can control how much overall budget is reserved for shared context (shared_context_budget_percent).
   - allocateTokenBudget accepts a customAllocation to override defaults per-call.

4. Inclusion logic
   - Within each bucket (project or each shared category), docs are sorted by relevanceScore then added until that bucket’s token limit is reached.
   - Docs that don’t fit are marked allocated: false with reason "exceeded budget".

5. Filters and parameters
   - rlm_shared_context supports max_tokens and categories filters (e.g., request only MANDATORY and BEST_PRACTICES).
   - Limiting which categories reduces what the allocator must fill.

6. Hard limits and truncation
   - Collections and docs are subject to hard limits (e.g., MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION = 100,000, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION = 50). If a collection exceeds token limits it’s truncated to fit before allocation.

Example
- totalBudget = 4000, using DEFAULT_ALLOCATION:
  - project budget = floor(4000 * 0.40) = 1600 tokens
  - MANDATORY = floor(4000 * 0.25) = 1000 tokens
  - BEST_PRACTICES = floor(4000 * 0.20) = 800 tokens
  - GUIDELINES = 400 tokens
  - REFERENCE = 200 tokens
  - Each bucket fills by relevance until its tokens are consumed.

Practical takeaway
- Project docs get first priority. Shared context is split by category weights and filled by relevance. You can tune overall shared percent (rlm_context_query) or pass custom allocations to change the split.

Short answer
- The query budget is split between shared context (team collections) and project-specific docs. Shared context is allocated a fixed percentage of the total token budget, and the remaining tokens go to project docs.
- Inside the shared slice, tokens are distributed by category priority (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Documents are then selected per-category until that category’s sub-budget is exhausted (sorted by relevance/priority).

Details / how it works
- Top-level split
  - Shared context is fetched first and given a configurable portion of the overall token budget (examples in the docs use 30% or 40% for shared context). The remaining budget is used to fetch project-specific documents.
  - You can control this with rlm_context_query parameters (include_shared_context and shared_context_budget_percent) or by calling rlm_shared_context(max_tokens=...).

- Category-level allocation (shared collections)
  - Shared documents are grouped into categories with different priorities:
    - MANDATORY — highest priority
    - BEST_PRACTICES — high
    - GUIDELINES — medium
    - REFERENCE — low
  - The docs describe canonical category percentages (example: MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10%). The implementation code similarly splits the shared slice among categories (and some codebases show slightly different defaults — these numbers are configurable).
  - Each category receives its percentage of the shared-context budget (or its percent of the total, depending on how the top-level split is applied). Documents in a category are picked until that category budget is used.

- Selection algorithm (implementation specifics)
  - The allocateTokenBudget function groups docs by source/category, computes per-group budgets, then calls allocateWithinBudget.
  - allocateWithinBudget sorts docs by relevanceScore (if present) and iteratively includes documents until the sub-budget is exhausted. Docs that would exceed the budget are marked allocated: false (allocationReason: 'exceeded budget').
  - You can supply a customAllocation to override default budget shares.

- Hard limits and safeguards
  - There are hard limits such as MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT, MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION, MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION, MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD.
  - enforceCollectionLimits truncates a collection to fit MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION if needed (keeps as many docs as will fit).

- What you’ll see in responses
  - rlm_shared_context often returns documents plus a budget_allocation object that shows how many tokens were assigned per category and the total token usage.

Quick numeric example
- If totalBudget = 4000 and you set shared_context_budget_percent = 30:
  - Shared context = 1,200 tokens, project docs = 2,800 tokens.
  - If shared categories use 40/30/20/10, then MANDATORY = 480, BEST_PRACTICES = 360, GUIDELINES = 240, REFERENCE = 120.
  - Documents are then chosen per-category until the category budgets are exhausted.

Control points for engineers
- Use rlm_context_query.shared_context_budget_percent and rlm_shared_context.max_tokens to change the split.
- Use allocateTokenBudget’s customAllocation (server-side) to adjust exact shares.
- Keep shared documents focused and prioritized so high-value MANDATORY/BEST_PRACTICES docs get included within their category budgets.

If you want, I can show how to compute the exact per-category token numbers for a specific totalBudget and shared percentage.

Short answer
- The system splits your query token budget between project docs and shared collections, then splits the shared portion by category (MANDATORY, BEST_PRACTICES, GUIDELINES, REFERENCE). Docs are included until each partition’s token budget is exhausted.

How it works (details)
1. Two layers of allocation
   - Project vs Shared: The token-budget logic first splits totalBudget between "project" and shared categories. By default the code’s DEFAULT_ALLOCATION is:
     - project: 40%
     - MANDATORY (shared): 25%
     - BEST_PRACTICES (shared): 20%
     - GUIDELINES (shared): 10%
     - REFERENCE (shared): 5%
   - Shared-category proportions (higher-level docs table / docs) commonly reported are MANDATORY 40%, BEST_PRACTICES 30%, GUIDELINES 20%, REFERENCE 10% — keep in mind these are the conceptual category priorities used by the product docs and some APIs; concrete budget values can differ in code/config and are configurable.

2. Query ordering vs allocation
   - rlm_context_query/rlm_shared_context: shared context is fetched/merged early in the query pipeline (the docs describe shared being considered first and configurable via shared_context_budget_percent).
   - Allocation logic (allocateTokenBudget): when deciding which documents to include, the implementation allocates the project partition first, then allocates each shared category partition. So project docs get a reserved portion of the budget and shared docs are filled per-category after that.

3. Per-document selection
   - Within each partition (project or a shared category), docs are sorted by relevanceScore (if present) and included until the partition’s token budget is used.
   - Documents that cannot fit are marked as not allocated ("exceeded budget").

4. Configurability
   - rlm_shared_context and rlm_context_query accept parameters:
     - max_tokens (total budget)
     - categories (which shared categories to include)
     - include_shared_context (bool)
     - shared_context_budget_percent (controls how much of the overall budget to reserve to shared context)
   - allocateTokenBudget accepts a customAllocation override so you can change the default splits programmatically.

5. Enforced limits and safeguards
   - MAX_COLLECTIONS_PER_PROJECT: 10
   - MAX_TOKENS_PER_COLLECTION: 100,000 (collection truncation logic will drop docs beyond this)
   - MAX_DOCS_PER_COLLECTION: 50
   - MIN_RELEVANCE_THRESHOLD: 0.2
   - Per-collection UI may show/allow tokenBudgetPercent overrides for linked collections.

Example (approx)
- totalBudget = 4000 and using the DEFAULT_ALLOCATION in the code:
  - projectBudget ≈ 0.40 * 4000 = 1600 tokens
  - MANDATORY ≈ 0.25 * 4000 = 1000 tokens
  - BEST_PRACTICES ≈ 800 tokens
  - GUIDELINES ≈ 400 tokens
  - REFERENCE ≈ 200 tokens
- The system will include project docs up to 1600 tokens (by relevance), then include shared docs from each category up to that category’s budget.

Practical notes
- If you need more shared context, set shared_context_budget_percent when calling rlm_context_query or supply customAllocation to allocateTokenBudget.
- MANDATORY rules are treated highest priority conceptually — make sure to include MANDATORY in your categories when you require non-negotiable rules.
- The pipeline both respects category priorities and uses per-doc relevance to choose the most useful material within each budget slice.

Short answer: Snipara does not include a built‑in WYSIWYG real‑time co‑editing editor. It’s a context‑optimization / indexing service, not a docs editor. That said, you can get robust near‑real‑time collaboration by pairing your team editor (Git, VS Code Live Share, HackMD, Notion, etc.) with Snipara’s sync APIs / GitHub integration and the VS Code extension. Below are practical options and step‑by‑step configs.

1) Recommended — Git + Snipara sync (best for teams)
- Use your normal Git + PR workflow for collaborative editing (GitHub/GitLab/Bitbucket).
- Enable Snipara GitHub sync (PRO+ / Team or self‑hosted). In the Snipara UI connect the repo / install the app so Snipara indexes changes automatically.
- For local/offline workflows, install the included post-commit hook (.git/hooks/post-commit) from the repo (the script in your docs). Configure:
  - Set SNIPARA_PROJECT_ID and SNIPARA_API_KEY (in .env, .env.local, or env vars).
  - Make the hook executable: chmod +x .git/hooks/post-commit
  - The hook will POST changed markdown files to SNIPARA_API_URL/projects/<PROJECT_ID>/documents.
- Advantages: familiar review/PR flow, atomic changes, auditability, fast indexing via webhooks.

2) Near‑real‑time in the editor — VS Code Live Share + Snipara VS Code extension
- Have collaborators use VS Code Live Share (real‑time editing).
- Install the snipara-vscode extension (packages/vscode-extension in repo). It provides commands: Ask Question, Search, Inject/Show/Clear Context and a Results/sidebar.
- When edits are finished, commit + push. Either rely on GitHub sync or use the post-commit hook to push docs to Snipara.
- This gives real‑time co-editing in the editor + fast indexing into Snipara for queries.

3) External collaborative editor + automated sync
- If you use HackMD/Notion/Confluence/etc., set up an automatic sync job (CI or scheduled worker) that calls Snipara’s documents API to upload changed content.
- Use the same API endpoint the hook uses:
  - POST /projects/<PROJECT_ID>/documents
  - Authorization: Bearer <SNIPARA_API_KEY>
  - Payload: JSON array of { path, content } entries (see hook script for example).
- Optionally run a small serverless function to convert/export documents and push them immediately on change.

4) Shared context collections (team knowledge)
- Snipara supports “shared context” collections (Team/Enterprise/self‑hosted).
- When you call rlm_context_query, include_shared is true by default; this ensures team-level collections are considered in context returned to LLMs.
- Use shared collections for canonical summaries, playbooks, and policies that you want included by all queries without duplicating in every doc.

5) Webhooks / indexing latency & best practices
- Use repo webhooks (recommended) or the post‑commit hook to trigger immediate indexing so queries see changes quickly.
- Keep each document reasonably sized (smart chunking) to improve retrieval.
- For high‑rate collaborative edits you may want a short debounce before pushing to Snipara to avoid indexing thrash.

Limitations / reality check
- Snipara is not a co‑editing UI — it optimizes and serves context. For true live cursors/OT/CRDT you must use an editor that supports real‑time collaboration (Live Share, HackMD, Google Docs) and then sync to Snipara.
- For team query results also ensure you use Team/Enterprise/shared collections to get unified shared content.

If you tell me which editor/workflow you want to use (GitHub PRs, VS Code + Live Share, HackMD, or something else) I can give a precise step‑by‑step config (example webhook settings, post‑commit hook customization, or a small sync script).

Short answer
Snipara doesn’t include its own collaborative editor; instead you enable “real‑time” collaboration by (1) turning on shared/team context in Snipara, (2) wiring an editor or CI hook to push changes into Snipara as they happen, and (3) using the MCP SSE endpoint (or the VS Code extension) so clients receive updates immediately.

How to configure it (step‑by‑step)
1) Plan & access
- Use TEAM, ENTERPRISE or SELF_HOSTED (these plans include shared context and team features). Enterprise/self‑hosted required for SSO/SAML & audit logs.

2) Make sure indexing/sync is enabled
- If you want edits to appear automatically, enable GitHub sync (if available for your plan) or install the provided Git hook (see .git/hooks/post-commit in the repo) to push changed markdown files to Snipara after each commit.
- For ad‑hoc/editor pushes the VS Code extension and API allow you to upload documents directly.

3) Enable shared collections
- Create shared context collections / team collections in the Snipara project and grant team permissions so multiple users write/read the same collections. The server’s context query supports include_shared and merged shared collections (see rlm_context_query include_shared).

4) Provide real‑time delivery (SSE/websocket)
- Configure the MCP SSE endpoint so clients can subscribe to live context events. Example Continue.dev config shows the SSE MCP transport:
  {
    "mcpServers": [
      {
        "name": "rlm",
        "transport": {
          "type": "sse",
          "url": "https://api.rlmsaas.com/v1/PROJECT_ID/mcp/sse",
          "headers": { "X-API-Key": "{{RLM_API_KEY}}" }
        }
      }
    ]
  }
- If self‑hosting, enable the MCP SSE transport on your mcp-server (the repo includes an SSE endpoint at /v1/{project_id}/mcp/sse).

5) Hook the editor/clients
- VS Code: install the snipara-vscode extension (supports Ask/Search/Inject/Show/Clear Context and session context). Configure API key + project ID in the extension settings.
- Other editors/web apps: either use a realtime collaboration layer (Live Share, CRDT/OT) for the actual editing + push content changes to Snipara via the documents API, or subscribe to the MCP SSE feed to receive context/index updates and rlm_context_query to request optimized context on demand.

6) Retrieval & usage
- Clients should call rlm_context_query (with include_shared=true) to get optimized merged context for the latest state.
- If you need immediate updates, subscribe to the MCP SSE feed so the client can re-run rlm_context_query or refresh injected session context when an update event arrives.

Example minimal architecture
- Editor (VS Code or web editor w/ CRDT) ←→ Snipara documents API (or post-commit hook/GitHub sync) → Snipara indexing
- Snipara MCP SSE → connected clients (VS Code extension / other MCP clients) → clients requery rlm_context_query to get new optimized context

If no relevant documents are found, the system returns a no-results response (not an error) and the UI should show a helpful empty state. Concretely:

- rlm_context_query will succeed but return no sections (sections = []), total_tokens = 0, truncated = false, and any session_context or suggestions it can generate.
- The frontend should show a friendly empty-state UI per the guidance: a clear message (e.g., "No matches found"), comfortable vertical padding (py-8), and one or more action buttons.
- Recommended actions to present to the user:
  - Broaden or reword the query (or switch search_mode: keyword/semantic/hybrid).
  - Check that docs are indexed (upload .md/.txt/.mdx at https://snipara.com/dashboard).
  - Run a decomposition/multi-query to break the question into smaller subqueries.
  - View related docs or global suggestions (if any).
- Example minimal response pattern (conceptual): { "sections": [], "total_tokens": 0, "truncated": false, "session_context": "...", "suggestions": [...] }

This keeps the experience informative and gives clear next steps instead of a blank screen.

If no relevant documents are found, the context-query tool and the UI fall back to an empty-state response instead of returning content. Concretely:

- rlm_context_query will succeed but return no sections (sections = []), total_tokens = 0, truncated = false, and usage/search fields describing what was scanned.
- The response will include helpful suggestions (e.g., rephrase the query, try keyword search, broaden search_mode, index/upload docs) and may include session_context if available.
- The UI should display an empty-state message (e.g., "No results found" or "No tasks available"), use padding (py-8), and present action buttons (rephrase, search settings, upload docs).

Minimal example response:
{
  "sections": [],
  "total_tokens": 0,
  "truncated": false,
  "session_context": null,
  "suggestions": [
    "Try a simpler or broader query",
    "Switch search_mode to 'keyword'",
    "Index your docs at https://snipara.com/dashboard",
    "Check spelling or include file paths"
  ]
}

Recommended next steps: rephrase or broaden your query, try keyword search mode, or upload/index the repository documentation so future queries can return relevant sections.

If no relevant documents are found, the context tool and UI fall back to an “empty state” instead of returning content. Concretely:

- rlm_context_query returns success with:
  - "sections": [] (empty list)
  - "total_tokens": 0
  - "truncated": false (since nothing filled the budget)
  - "session_context": possibly present
  - "suggestions": helpful next steps (e.g., broaden the query, check indexing, try keyword mode)
  - usage/diagnostics still present (search_time_ms, sections_scanned, cache_hit)

- Other tools follow the same pattern (e.g., decompose returns no sub_queries, example responses show message like "No tasks available").

- UI behavior: show a friendly empty-state (helpful message, action buttons such as “Upload docs”, “Retry with different query”, “Switch to keyword search”), use layout padding (py-8) and avoid fixed heights.

Actionable things you can try when you hit an empty state:
- Verify your docs are indexed in the dashboard (upload .md/.txt/.mdx).
- Broaden or rephrase the query, or switch search_mode (keyword/semantic/hybrid).
- Increase max_tokens or include session_id to use prior context.
- Check indexing logs or cache_hit/sections_scanned diagnostics.
